description.summary: In Amazonian folklore, the Boto Cor-de-Rosa — the pink Amazon river dolphin (Inia geoffrensis) — is said to transform at night into a handsome man who seduces women at riverside parties before returning to the water at dawn.
description.full: The Boto Cor-de-Rosa, or pink river dolphin, is a shapeshifting being of Amazonian folklore centered on the Amazon river dolphin Inia geoffrensis. According to traditions held by riverine communities along the Amazon and its tributaries — particularly in the Brazilian states of Amazonas and Pará but extending into Peru, Colombia, Bolivia, and Venezuela — the dolphin emerges from the river at dusk and transforms into a tall, handsome, well-dressed young man. Almost always he wears a hat to conceal the blowhole that does not disappear with the transformation. He attends festas (riverside parties), dances exceptionally well, seduces a young woman, and returns to the water before sunrise. The folklore complex serves several social functions: it provides a culturally legible explanation for unwanted or unexplained pregnancies (a fatherless child is often called a 'filho do boto'), enforces taboos against entering the water alone between dusk and dawn, and frames the boto as one of many encantados — enchanted beings able to move between human and animal form. Within the broader encantado tradition the boto is also credited with controlling storms, causing illness or madness, and drawing humans into the underwater realm. The myth has direct conservation consequences: in some communities reverence and fear protect dolphins, while in others (notably documented since the 2000s in the upper Amazon) dolphin parts have been killed for use as piracatinga catfish bait or sold as love charms.

etymology.origin: 'Boto' is the common Portuguese term for several South American river dolphins; 'cor-de-rosa' is Portuguese for 'pink-coloured', the diagnostic adult pigmentation of Inia geoffrensis. The term 'encantado' ('enchanted one') is the broader category to which the boto belongs in Amazonian folk religion.
etymology.meaning: 'Pink dolphin' / 'pink boto'. The pinkening of older adults is caused by abrasion-related scarring of the skin.
etymology.first_use: Portuguese-language usage attested in Brazilian folklore collections from the 19th century onward.

related_phenomena.name: Encantado
related_phenomena.description: Broader Amazonian category of enchanted shapeshifting beings; the boto is one type

related_phenomena.name: Iara
related_phenomena.description: Female water-spirit of Brazilian folklore who lures men into the river

related_phenomena.name: Kelpie
related_phenomena.description: Scottish water-horse shapeshifter associated with drowning

coloration: pink (in dolphin form, intensifying with age)

features.description: In human form a hole or scar remains on the top of the head; almost universally concealed by a wide-brimmed white hat
Tall, charming, exceptionally well-dressed (often in a white suit) and an excellent dancer
Adult Inia geoffrensis develop a pink hue from scar tissue accumulating with age

physical.morphology: In its animal form the boto is the freshwater cetacean Inia geoffrensis: an unfused-cervical-vertebra dolphin able to flex its neck, with a long beak, paddle-like flippers, and a low dorsal ridge. In transformed form it is described as an idealized human male; female-boto traditions, in which a woman emerges from the river to seduce men, are also recorded but rarer.

abilities.description: Transformation between dolphin and human form, typically at dusk and reverting at dawn
Charisma, dancing skill, and supernatural attractiveness used to seduce festa-goers
Believed to summon storms and disturb the river when angered
Encantados, including the boto, are blamed for sudden illness, fevers, miscarriage, and mental disturbances
Reputed to draw humans (especially women) into an enchanted city beneath the river

behavior.diet: Inia geoffrensis is piscivorous (fish, including armored catfish and piranha) and crustacean-feeding.
patterns: Emerges from the river at dusk in human form
Wears a hat to hide the blowhole
Returns to the water before sunrise
Said to be especially active during the June Festas Juninas season

range.description: The folklore tracks the natural range of Inia geoffrensis: the Amazon and Orinoco basins of South America. The pink-river-dolphin shapeshifter is most strongly associated with the Brazilian Amazon, with parallel encantado traditions in the Peruvian, Colombian, Bolivian, and Venezuelan Amazon.

hotspots.name: Mid-Solimões / Tefé

location.description: Mamirauá Sustainable Development Reserve and surrounding várzea

hotspots.peak_period: Year-round; folklore most active in June festas season
hotspots.notes: Long-running site of dolphin ecological research and dense boto folklore.

hotspots.name: Parintins
hotspots.notes: The annual Boi-Bumbá festival prominently features boto and other Amazonian folkloric figures.

first_recorded.source: Brazilian folklore collections; oral tradition predates European contact

peak_activity_periods.description: Continuous folkloric tradition documented in Portuguese-language ethnography
Scholarly attention and conservation discussion (Slater, Cravalho)

temporal.seasonal_patterns: Stories cluster around the June Festas Juninas, when riverside dances most plausibly draw a 'stranger in a white suit'.

origins.cultural_roots: Indigenous Amazonian (Tupi, Tukano, Kokama, and others) cosmologies that frame the river as a populated otherworld; merged in the colonial era with Portuguese folklore traditions about enchanted strangers and water-spirits.
origins.earliest_accounts: Oral; written documentation begins with Brazilian folklore collectors of the 19th and early 20th centuries (e.g., Câmara Cascudo).
origins.folklore_connections: Part of the encantado complex; cognate with global water-shapeshifter traditions including the Scottish selkie, the Irish kelpie, and the Slavic rusalka.

timeline.event: Indigenous Amazonian traditions of dolphin-people and underwater enchanted cities
timeline.significance: Cultural baseline of the boto encantado complex

timeline.event: Luís da Câmara Cascudo publishes 'Dicionário do Folclore Brasileiro', cataloguing boto entries
timeline.significance: Major reference text for Brazilian folklore including boto

timeline.event: Candace Slater publishes 'Dance of the Dolphin: Transformation and Disenchantment in the Amazonian Imagination'
timeline.significance: Foundational English-language ethnography of boto folklore

history.modern_era: The boto myth survives strongly in contemporary Amazonia and has become a regional cultural icon — featured at the Festival de Parintins, in school curricula, and in conservation campaigning by groups such as the Mamirauá Institute and WWF. At the same time it intersects fraught conservation realities: in the upper Amazon piracatinga ('mota' catfish) fisheries, river dolphins have been killed for bait, prompting Brazilian fishing-moratorium decrees beginning in 2014.

documentary.name: Dicionário do Folclore Brasileiro
documentary.description: Reference entries on 'boto' and 'encantado' in Câmara Cascudo's encyclopedic dictionary of Brazilian folklore.

documentary.name: Dance of the Dolphin
documentary.description: Book-length ethnographic study of boto folklore in the central Amazon by Candace Slater.
documentary.analysis: Widely cited academic treatment of the boto as encantado.

paranormal.name: Boto as encantado
paranormal.description: The traditional Amazonian view: the boto is one form of an encantado, an enchanted being that moves between the underwater otherworld and the world of humans.
evidence_cited: Ritual practices around births and water taboos

skeptical.name: Social-explanatory function
skeptical.description: Anthropologists analyse the myth as a culturally legible cover story for unwanted pregnancies and a vehicle for water-safety taboos in environments where drowning is common.
evidence_cited: Use of 'filho do boto' for fatherless children
Geographic correlation of warnings with high-risk river crossings

skeptical.name: Misperception of dolphin behaviour
skeptical.description: Inia geoffrensis is naturally curious, will approach swimmers, and surfaces audibly at night; cetologists argue these behaviours plausibly seed the human-encounter narrative.
proponents: Cetacean researchers (e.g., Vera da Silva, Mamirauá Institute)
evidence_cited: Documented inquisitive behaviour of Inia toward boats and swimmers

traditions: Festa-going boto in white suit and hat
Female-boto ('boto fêmea') variant who seduces men
stories: The festa stranger who leaves before dawn
The young woman drawn into the underwater city
Origin stories of children attributed to the boto
folklore.beliefs: Many riverine communities forbid swimming alone at dusk or dawn, avoid eating boto meat, and treat the dolphin as a being deserving of caution and respect.

indigenous.significance: The pink dolphin features in Indigenous and caboclo cosmologies as a being that crosses between the human world and the river otherworld; its protection is in some communities a culturally enforced norm.
indigenous.sensitivity_note: Encantado traditions are living religious frameworks for many Amazonian communities, not merely 'folklore'.
indigenous.respect_guidelines: Engage Indigenous and caboclo voices when documenting the tradition; distinguish ethnographic framings from sensationalised cryptid framings.

literature: Dance of the Dolphin: Transformation and Disenchantment in the Amazonian Imagination — Candace Slater (1994)
Dicionário do Folclore Brasileiro — Luís da Câmara Cascudo (1954)
film_tv: Recurring character in Brazilian telenovelas set in Amazonia
music: Boto cor-de-rosa figures recurrently in Brazilian regional music, including Boi-Bumbá songs at the Festival de Parintins
other: WWF and Mamirauá Institute conservation campaigns invoking boto folklore

researchers.name: Luís da Câmara Cascudo
researchers.contribution: Catalogued boto entries in the canonical 'Dicionário do Folclore Brasileiro'

researchers.name: Candace Slater
researchers.contribution: Ethnographic study of boto folklore in 'Dance of the Dolphin' (1994)

researchers.name: Vera M. F. da Silva
researchers.contribution: Cetacean biology of Inia geoffrensis; conservation research overlapping the folklore

organizations.name: Mamirauá Sustainable Development Institute
organizations.focus: Amazon river dolphin research and community conservation

organizations.name: WWF Brasil — Boto initiative
organizations.focus: Conservation of Amazon and Araguaian river dolphins

research.ongoing_studies: Cetacean ecology and population studies of Inia geoffrensis, ethnographic work on encantado traditions, and applied research linking folkloric reverence to dolphin conservation outcomes.

sources.title: Dance of the Dolphin: Transformation and Disenchantment in the Amazonian Imagination
